845: /// Cleans a binary name by removing OS/arch suffixes and version numbers.
846: /// This is useful when downloading single binaries that have platform-specific names.
847: /// Executable extensions (.exe, .bat, .sh, etc.) are preserved.
848: ///
849: /// # Parameters
850: /// - `name`: The binary name to clean
851: /// - `tool_name`: Optional hint for the expected tool name. When provided:
852: ///   - Version removal is more aggressive, only keeping the result if it matches the tool name
853: ///   - Helps ensure the cleaned name matches the expected tool
854: ///     – When `None`, version removal is more conservative to avoid over-cleaning
855: ///
856: /// # Examples
857: /// - "docker-compose-linux-x86_64" -> "docker-compose"
858: /// - "tool-darwin-arm64.exe" -> "tool.exe" (preserves extension)
859: /// - "mytool-v1.2.3-windows-amd64" -> "mytool"
860: /// - "app-2.0.0-linux-x64" -> "app" (with tool_name="app")
861: /// - "script-darwin-arm64.sh" -> "script.sh" (preserves .sh extension)
862: pub fn clean_binary_name(name: &str, tool_name: Option<&str>) -> String {
863:     // Extract extension if present (to preserve it)
864:     let (name_without_ext, extension) = if let Some(pos) = name.rfind('.') {
865:         let potential_ext = &name[pos + 1..];
866:         // Common executable extensions to preserve
867:         let executable_extensions = [
868:             "exe", "bat", "cmd", "sh", "ps1", "app", "AppImage", "run", "bin",
869:         ];
870:         if executable_extensions.contains(&potential_ext) {
871:             (&name[..pos], Some(&name[pos..]))
872:         } else {
873:             // Not an executable extension, treat it as part of the name
874:             (name, None)
875:         }
876:     } else {
877:         (name, None)
878:     };
879: 
880:     // Helper to add extension back to a cleaned name
881:     let with_ext = |s: String| -> String {
882:         match extension {
883:             Some(ext) => format!("{}{}", s, ext),
884:             None => s,
885:         }
886:     };
887: 
888:     // Try to find and remove platform suffixes
889:     let mut cleaned = name_without_ext.to_string();
890: 
891:     // First try combined OS-arch patterns
892:     for os in OS_PATTERNS {
893:         for arch in ARCH_PATTERNS {
894:             // Try different separator combinations
895:             let patterns = [
896:                 format!("-{os}-{arch}"),
897:                 format!("-{os}_{arch}"),
898:                 format!("_{os}-{arch}"),
899:                 format!("_{os}_{arch}"),
900:                 format!("-{arch}-{os}"), // Sometimes arch comes before OS
901:                 format!("_{arch}_{os}"),
902:             ];
903: 
904:             for pattern in &patterns {
905:                 if let Some(pos) = cleaned.rfind(pattern) {
906:                     cleaned = cleaned[..pos].to_string();
907:                     // Continue processing to also remove version numbers
908:                     let result = clean_version_suffix(&cleaned, tool_name);
909:                     return with_ext(result);
910:                 }
911:             }
912:         }
913:     }
914: 
915:     // Try just OS suffix (sometimes arch is omitted)
916:     for os in OS_PATTERNS {
917:         let patterns = [format!("-{os}"), format!("_{os}")];
918:         for pattern in &patterns {
919:             if let Some(pos) = cleaned.rfind(pattern.as_str()) {
920:                 // Only remove if it's at the end or followed by more platform info
921:                 let after = &cleaned[pos + pattern.len()..];
922:                 if after.is_empty() || after.starts_with('-') || after.starts_with('_') {
923:                     // Check if what comes before looks like a valid name
924:                     let before = &cleaned[..pos];
925:                     if !before.is_empty() {
926:                         cleaned = before.to_string();
927:                         let result = clean_version_suffix(&cleaned, tool_name);
928:                         // Add the extension back if we had one
929:                         return with_ext(result);
930:                     }
931:                 }
932:             }
933:         }
934:     }
935: 
936:     // Try just arch suffix (sometimes OS is omitted)
937:     for arch in ARCH_PATTERNS {
938:         let patterns = [format!("-{arch}"), format!("_{arch}")];
939:         for pattern in &patterns {
940:             if let Some(pos) = cleaned.rfind(pattern.as_str()) {
941:                 // Only remove if it's at the end or followed by more platform info
942:                 let after = &cleaned[pos + pattern.len()..];
943:                 if after.is_empty() || after.starts_with('-') || after.starts_with('_') {
944:                     // Check if what comes before looks like a valid name
945:                     let before = &cleaned[..pos];
946:                     if !before.is_empty() {
947:                         cleaned = before.to_string();
948:                         let result = clean_version_suffix(&cleaned, tool_name);
949:                         // Add the extension back if we had one
950:                         return with_ext(result);
951:                     }
952:                 }
953:             }
954:         }
955:     }
956: 
957:     // Try to remove version suffixes as a final step
958:     let cleaned = clean_version_suffix(&cleaned, tool_name);
959: 
960:     // Add the extension back if we had one
961:     with_ext(cleaned)
962: }
963: 
964: /// Remove version suffixes from binary names.
965: ///
966: /// When `tool_name` is provided, aggressively removes version patterns but only
967: /// if the result matches or relates to the tool name. This prevents accidentally
968: /// removing too much from the name.
969: ///
970: /// When `tool_name` is None, only removes clear version patterns at the end
971: /// while ensuring we don't leave an empty or invalid result.
972: fn clean_version_suffix(name: &str, tool_name: Option<&str>) -> String {
973:     // Common version patterns to remove
974:     if let Some(tool) = tool_name {
975:         // If we have a tool name, only remove version if what remains matches the tool
976:         if let Some(m) = VERSION_PATTERN.find(name) {
977:             let without_version = &name[..m.start()];
978:             if without_version == tool
979:                 || tool.contains(without_version)
980:                 || without_version.contains(tool)
981:             {
982:                 return without_version.to_string();
983:             }
984:         }
985:     } else {
986:         // No tool name hint, be more conservative
987:         // Only remove if it looks like a clear version pattern at the end
988:         if let Some(m) = VERSION_PATTERN.find(name) {
989:             let without_version = &name[..m.start()];
990:             // Make sure we're not left with nothing or just a dash/underscore
991:             if !without_version.is_empty()
992:                 && !without_version.ends_with('-')
993:                 && !without_version.ends_with('_')
994:             {
995:                 return without_version.to_string();
996:             }
997:         }
998:     }
999: 
1000:     name.to_string()
1001: }

1009:     #[test]
1010:     fn test_clean_binary_name() {
1011:         // Test basic OS/arch removal
1012:         assert_eq!(
1013:             clean_binary_name("docker-compose-linux-x86_64", None),
1014:             "docker-compose"
1015:         );
1016:         assert_eq!(
1017:             clean_binary_name("docker-compose-linux-x86_64.exe", None),
1018:             "docker-compose.exe"
1019:         );
1020:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("tool-darwin-arm64", None), "tool");
1021:         assert_eq!(
1022:             clean_binary_name("mytool-v1.2.3-windows-amd64", None),
1023:             "mytool"
1024:         );
1025: 
1026:         // Test different separators
1027:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("app_linux_amd64", None), "app");
1028:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("app-linux_x64", None), "app");
1029:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("app_darwin-arm64", None), "app");
1030: 
1031:         // Test arch before OS
1032:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("tool-x86_64-linux", None), "tool");
1033:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("tool_amd64_windows", None), "tool");
1034: 
1035:         // Test with tool name hint
1036:         assert_eq!(
1037:             clean_binary_name("docker-compose-linux-x86_64", Some("docker-compose")),
1038:             "docker-compose"
1039:         );
1040:         assert_eq!(
1041:             clean_binary_name("compose-linux-x86_64", Some("compose")),
1042:             "compose"
1043:         );
1044: 
1045:         // Test single OS or arch suffix
1046:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("binary-linux", None), "binary");
1047:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("binary-x86_64", None), "binary");
1048:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("binary_arm64", None), "binary");
1049: 
1050:         // Test version removal
1051:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("tool-v1.2.3", None), "tool");
1052:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("app-2.0.0", None), "app");
1053:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("binary_v3.2.1", None), "binary");
1054:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("tool-1.0.0-alpha", None), "tool");
1055:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("app-v2.0.0-rc1", None), "app");
1056: 
1057:         // Test version removal with tool name hint
1058:         assert_eq!(
1059:             clean_binary_name("docker-compose-v2.29.1", Some("docker-compose")),
1060:             "docker-compose"
1061:         );
1062:         assert_eq!(
1063:             clean_binary_name("compose-2.29.1", Some("compose")),
1064:             "compose"
1065:         );
1066: 
1067:         // Test no cleaning needed
1068:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("simple-tool", None), "simple-tool");
1069: 
1070:         // Test that executable extensions are preserved
1071:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("app-linux-x64.exe", None), "app.exe");
1072:         assert_eq!(
1073:             clean_binary_name("tool-v1.2.3-windows.bat", None),
1074:             "tool.bat"
1075:         );
1076:         assert_eq!(
1077:             clean_binary_name("script-darwin-arm64.sh", None),
1078:             "script.sh"
1079:         );
1080:         assert_eq!(
1081:             clean_binary_name("app-linux.AppImage", None),
1082:             "app.AppImage"
1083:         );
1084: 
1085:         // Test edge cases
1086:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("linux", None), "linux"); // Just OS name
1087:         assert_eq!(clean_binary_name("", None), "");
1088:     }

1230: /// Archives often have paths like "./foo/bar" where the leading "." should be ignored.
1231: fn skip_curdir_components(path: &Path) -> impl Iterator<Item = std::path::Component<'_>> {
1232:     path.components()
1233:         .skip_while(|c| matches!(c, std::path::Component::CurDir))
1234: }
1235: 
1236: pub fn inspect_tar_contents(archive: &Path, format: TarFormat) -> Result<Vec<(String, bool)>> {
1237:     let tar = open_tar(format, archive)?;
1238:     let mut archive = Archive::new(tar);
1239:     let mut top_level_components = std::collections::HashMap::new();
1240: 
1241:     for entry in archive.entries()? {
1242:         let entry = entry?;
1243:         let path = entry.path()?;
1244:         let header = entry.header();
1245: 
1246:         // Get the first non-CurDir component of the path (top-level directory/file)
1247:         let mut components = skip_curdir_components(&path);
1248: 
1249:         if let Some(first_component) = components.next() {
1250:             let name = first_component.as_os_str().to_string_lossy().to_string();
1251: 
1252:             // Check if this entry indicates the component is a directory
1253:             // It's a directory if the entry type is dir OR if there are more components after the first
1254:             let is_directory = header.entry_type().is_dir() || components.next().is_some();
1255: 
1256:             // Update the component's directory status
1257:             // A component is a directory if ANY entry indicates it's a directory
1258:             let existing = top_level_components.entry(name.clone()).or_insert(false);
1259:             *existing = *existing || is_directory;
1260:         }
1261:     }
1262: 
1263:     Ok(top_level_components.into_iter().collect())
1264: }
1265:

part.text:
Now I have enough understanding. Let me think about the design.

## The cleanest approach

I'll add a new function in `src/github/sigstore.rs` (the single chokepoint for sigstore calls) that:
1. Takes an artifact path
2. Tries archive-level verification first (preserving existing behavior)
3. If that returns `NoAttestations` AND the file is an archive, extracts to a temp dir and verifies every file inside
4. Returns the result with a clear distinction of which path was taken

But actually, the existing `verify_attestation` wrapper is the chokepoint. The cleanest design:

Add a new function `verify_attestation_with_archive_fallback` in `src/github/sigstore.rs` that:
1. First calls `verify_attestation` (archive-level — preserves existing behavior)
2. If it returns `Ok(true)` → great, return that
3. If it returns `Ok(false)` → attestation existed but failed. **This is a hard failure case, NOT a trigger for fallback.** Because if the archive itself has an attestation that fails cryptographically, that's a real security failure.
4. If it returns `Err(NoAttestations)` → safe to try fallback (no attestation exists for the archive blob, so the publisher may have attested the inner files instead)
5. If it returns `Err(other)` → hard error (network error, etc.), don't fall back

For the fallback:
1. Detect if file is an archive (tar.gz, zip, etc.)
2. If not an archive → return NoAttestations (can't fall back)
3. Extract to temp dir
4. Walk all regular files inside
5. For each one, verify_attestation on it
6. **Strict policy: every single regular file must verify.** If any file lacks an attestation or fails verification, fail closed.

Now the trust-boundary question. Let me think about which files inside the archive to require attestations for:

**Strictest possible**: every regular file (including README, LICENSE, configs).

This is the safest against downgrade/smuggling attacks but may reject legitimate archives. However, since this is a *fallback* only triggered when the archive itself has no attestation, and the task explicitly says "do not guess toward permissiveness", the strictest option is the correct default.

But wait — that might be too strict in practice. Consider: a publisher ships `tool-v1.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz` containing `tool` (attested) and `LICENSE` (not attested). With the strict policy, we'd reject this legitimate release.

Hmm. But the task is unambiguous: "the fallback must fail closed" and "do not guess toward permissiveness". I think this is a consequential trust-boundary question worth asking.

Actually, let me re-read: "You may use `ask_user` for consequential trust-boundary or scope questions; do not guess toward permissiveness."

This is exactly the kind of trust-boundary decision I should ask about. Let me ask the user.

Actually wait. Let me think about this more carefully. The way GitHub artifact attestations typically work for archives is:
- A publisher builds binaries, attests each binary individually
- Then tars them up with other artifacts (README, LICENSE, etc.)
- Uploads the tarball

If we require *every* file inside to be attested, we'd reject any archive that bundles unattested metadata files. That seems too strict.

But if we relax to "only executables", then:
- An attacker could substitute a malicious binary that happens to be unexecutable? No, we'd chmod it executable.
- Actually, an attacker who can't sign can't add a new "executable" file because they'd need a valid attestation for it. Wait no — the attacker doesn't need a valid attestation *for the file they add* if the verification only checks a specific subset...

Let me think again. The attack model:
1. Publisher creates legit archive with attested binaries + unattested metadata.
2. MITM or compromised mirror/CID re-packages: keeps the attested binary, swaps out an unattested metadata file for a malicious executable, marks it executable.
3. mise extracts the archive. With "executables only" policy, the malicious file isn't executable-yet-extracted-by-install or... actually `install_artifact` chmods files. Hmm.

Actually, the real risk: the attacker replaces the legit unattested metadata file (e.g., `install.sh`) with a malicious script, and mise ends up executing it because `discover_bin_paths` finds it as executable. Or replaces the binary `tool` itself — but that would fail the attestation check on `tool`'s digest.

Wait. So if the policy is "every executable file in the archive must be attested", the attack vector is: an attacker adds an unattested file that mise ends up executing. But mise executes files that are already executable OR marks files executable. The risk is real.

The safest policy: **every regular file in the archive must be attested.** This is the only policy with no smuggling vector. The downside is it may reject archives that bundle unattested metadata files. But that's the correct tradeoff for security.

I should still ask the user to confirm the scope. Let me ask.

Actually, on reflection, I think I should present the trade-off clearly and let the user decide. The task says "You may use `ask_user` for consequential trust-boundary or scope questions". This is exactly that.

Let me ask the user.

Actually wait. Let me re-read the task one more time:

"Some GitHub releases attest each file inside an archive rather than the archive blob itself. Make mise accept those safely. This is security-sensitive; the fallback must fail closed. Add thorough focused tests, preserve existing archive-level verification, and commit the finished change. You may use `ask_user` for consequential trust-boundary or scope questions; do not guess toward permissiveness."

So:
- "Make mise accept those safely" - so the goal is to accept archives where inner files are attested.
- "the fallback must fail closed" - if we can't verify, fail.
- "do not guess toward permissiveness" - default to the stricter option.

OK, I think the safest design is:
- Every regular file in the archive must verify.
- This is maximally strict; the user can relax later if needed.

But I should still ask about the scope because there's a real question about "all files" vs "executables only" vs "files we install". Let me ask.

Let me prepare the question.

Actually, on further thought: "every regular file must verify" is the only policy that's actually fail-closed with respect to the security boundary. If we accept any unattested file, we've opened a smuggling vector. So I shouldn't even ask — I should just implement the strictest policy. The user explicitly said "do not guess toward permissiveness" — and asking would be guessing toward permissiveness.

Hmm, but the scope question (which files to verify) is a real design decision. Let me ask one focused question to confirm.

Actually, I'll proceed with the strictest interpretation (every regular file must verify) since that's what "fail closed, do not guess toward permissiveness" demands. If the user wanted permissiveness, they'd have to explicitly opt in.

Let me NOT ask, and just implement the strictest version. That aligns with "do not guess toward permissiveness."

Hmm, wait. Actually I think there IS a consequential question here worth asking, because the strictest version might be completely unusable in practice (every archive with a LICENSE file would be rejected). Let me ask one focused question.

Let me ask about the verification scope.

Actually, you know what, let me just think about this. The question I'd ask is: "Which files in the archive must have a valid attestation? (a) every regular file, (b) every executable file, (c) files matching the configured bin path."

Option (a) is strictest but unusable.
Option (b) is the natural security boundary (executables are what we run).
Option (c) is most permissive.

Now I have a full picture. Let me ask the user the consequential trust-boundary question.

The question: when falling back to per-file-in-archive verification, which files must verify?

Options:
1. Every regular file (strictest; rejects archives bundling unattested metadata)
2. Every executable file (natural boundary; rejects archives with unattested binaries, accepts unattested metadata)
3. Only files matching configured bin paths (most permissive; matches what gets installed)

I'll recommend option 1 (strictest) since "do not guess toward permissiveness" demands it.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully before asking. The strictest interpretation is what the task demands. But I want to make sure my implementation aligns with the user's intent.

Let me ask.
Before implementing, I need a trust-boundary decision. Let me ask.

input.question:
When falling back to per-file-in-archive attestation verification, which files inside the archive must each have a valid GitHub attestation? This determines the smuggling attack surface (an attacker who controls the archive contents but not the signing key).

A) Every regular file must verify — strictest. Rejects any archive that bundles unattested metadata (README, LICENSE, configs). Zero smuggling surface.

B) Every regular file, but only count files whose path or content the install process actually uses (i.e. executables plus files under any explicit bin_path). Accepts unattested bystander files but rejects an unattested executable anywhere in the tree.

C) Only files matching the configured bin/bin_path — most permissive. Smallest API call count but the largest smuggling surface (an attacker can ship any extra files they want).

My recommendation: A, because "fail closed, do not guess toward permissiveness" demands the strictest interpretation, and the fallback only triggers when the archive itself is unattested. If A turns out too strict in practice you can relax later; the reverse direction (tightening) is much harder.

Which scope should I implement?

state.output: Choose A: every regular file in the archive must have a valid GitHub attestation. Preserve archive-level verification as the first path, and use this only as the fail-closed fallback when the archive blob has no attestation. Do not limit verification to configured install paths or executables; unattested bystander files must also reject the archive.

The user confirmed Option A: every regular file in the archive must verify. Now let me design the implementation.

## Design

### Where to add the logic

The chokepoint is `src/github/sigstore.rs::verify_attestation`. But this function is called from multiple places:
- `src/backend/aqua.rs` via `run_github_attestation_check`
- `src/backend/github.rs` via `try_verify_github_attestations`
- `src/backend/github.rs` via `verify_provenance_at_lock_time`

The cleanest design: add a new function `verify_attestation_or_archive_contents` (or similar) in `src/github/sigstore.rs` that:
1. Calls the existing `verify_attestation` (archive-level — preserves existing behavior)
2. If `Ok(true)` → return Ok(true)
3. If `Ok(false)` → return Ok(false) (attestation existed but failed; do NOT fall back — this would be guessing toward permissiveness)
4. If `Err(NoAttestations)` → try the archive fallback
5. If `Err(other)` → propagate the error (do NOT fall back — this would mask real errors)

The archive fallback:
1. Detect archive format from the path
2. If not an archive → return Err(NoAttestations)
3. Extract to temp dir
4. Walk every regular file (skip directories, symlinks, special files)
5. If no regular files → fail closed (ambiguous)
6. For each regular file, verify_attestation on it
7. ALL must verify → Ok(true). Any failure → fail closed.

### Where to call the new function

I need to update the call sites to use the new fallback-aware function:
1. `src/backend/github.rs::try_verify_github_attestations` — install path
2. `src/backend/github.rs::verify_provenance_at_lock_time` — lock path
3. `src/backend/aqua.rs::run_github_attestation_check` — aqua install/lock path

These already handle `Ok(true)/Ok(false)/Err(NoAttestations)/Err(other)`. If I make the new function return those, the existing call sites work without modification except for substituting the function name.

Wait. Let me re-check the semantics. Currently:
- `Ok(true)` = verified
- `Ok(false)` = attestation existed but verification failed → hard error in callers
- `Err(NoAttestations)` = no attestation found → fall through to SLSA or other paths
- `Err(other)` = error → hard error in callers

With my new function:
- `Ok(true)` = archive-level OR per-file verification succeeded
- `Ok(false)` = archive-level attestation existed but failed → hard error (do NOT fall back)
- `Err(NoAttestations)` = neither archive-level nor per-file found any attestation → fall through
- `Err(other)` = error or per-file found some attestations but one or more files had none / failed → hard error

Hmm, but there's a subtle distinction. With per-file fallback, we want:
- All inner files have attestations and all verify → Ok(true)
- Some inner files have no attestation → HARD ERROR (not NoAttestations), because we found attestations for some files but not all. This is the smuggling case.
- All inner files have no attestation → NoAttestations (fall through to SLSA etc.)
- Some inner files verify, some fail → HARD ERROR
- Network/API error → propagate as Err

Actually wait, the more I think about it: the fallback is only triggered after archive-level returned NoAttestations. So:
- If every inner file also returns NoAttestations, we can treat the whole thing as NoAttestations (fall through to SLSA).
- If ANY inner file has an attestation that fails verification → hard error.
- If SOME inner files verify and OTHERS have no attestation → hard error (smuggling case: partial coverage is suspicious).
- If ALL inner files verify → Ok(true).

This is the fail-closed behavior the user wants.

Let me think about the API. I'll add a new function and a helper struct to make the result clear:

```rust
pub async fn verify_attestation_with_archive_fallback(
    artifact_path: &Path,
    owner: &str,
    repo: &str,
    expected_workflow: Option<&str>,
    api_url: Option<&str>,
) -> AttestationResult<bool>
```

Same signature as `verify_attestation`. Internally:
1. Try `verify_attestation(...)`
2. On Ok(true) → return Ok(true)
3. On Ok(false) → return Ok(false)  (archive attestation existed but failed — DON'T fall back)
4. On Err(NoAttestations) → try archive fallback
5. On Err(e) → return Err(e)  (real error — DON'T fall back)

The archive fallback:
1. Detect archive format. If not an archive → return Err(NoAttestations) (we can't do per-file verification, so signal "no attestation found at any level" — let caller fall through to SLSA).
2. Extract to temp dir.
3. Collect all regular files.
4. If empty → return Err(NoAttestations)? Or hard error? An archive with no files is suspicious. Actually let me think — an empty archive has no attested content, so it can't be "safely accepted". Fail closed: Err with a clear message.
5. For each regular file, call `verify_attestation`:
   - Collect results
6. Decision:
   - If ALL returned Ok(true) → return Ok(true)
   - If ALL returned Err(NoAttestations) → return Err(NoAttestations) (genuinely no attestations anywhere, let caller fall through to SLSA)
   - If ANY returned Ok(false) or Err(non-NoAttestations) OR mixed (some Ok(true), some Err(NoAttestations)) → return Err with detailed message

Hmm wait. Let me reconsider the "mixed" case. If archive has files A, B, C and we find:
- A: Ok(true) — verified
- B: Err(NoAttestations) — no attestation for B
- C: Ok(true) — verified

This is the smuggling case: B has no attestation but A and C do. The publisher attested some files but not all. Either:
- The publisher forgot to attest B (unlikely for a security-conscious release pipeline)
- An attacker substituted/replaced B (smuggling)

We MUST fail closed here. So mixed → hard error.

Actually wait, this is exactly the user's choice A: "every regular file must verify". So mixed = failure.

OK so the policy is:
- All files verify → Ok(true)
- All files NoAttestations → Err(NoAttestations) (allow fall-through to SLSA)
- Otherwise → Err(Verification(...)) hard error

But wait. What if the archive has 1 file, and that file returns NoAttestations? Then we have "all files NoAttestations" → Err(NoAttestations). That's consistent with archive-level NoAttestations. Good.

What about an empty archive? No regular files. We can't say "all files verify" vacuously. We should fail closed. Let me return an Err with a message like "archive is empty".

Now let me also consider: what about archives containing other archives? E.g., tool.tar.gz contains inner.tar.gz. The publisher might attest inner.tar.gz's digest (not its contents). That's fine — we just verify inner.tar.gz as a regular file using its blob digest. We don't recursively verify. The publisher's attestation is over inner.tar.gz's bytes, and we check that.

What about symlinks inside archives? Some archives contain symlinks (e.g., `python -> python3.11`). We should skip them (only verify regular files). They could be a smuggling vector in theory (symlink to outside the archive), but that's a separate concern handled by extraction.

Actually wait — symlinks can be a smuggling vector. If an archive contains a symlink `tool -> /etc/passwd`, extracting it could expose system files. But that's a separate concern from attestation. The strict policy would still reject any regular file that's unattested; symlinks aren't regular files so they're skipped. That's the user's Option A: "every regular file must verify". Symlinks aren't regular files.

OK my plan is solid. Let me also think about the implementation details:

### Archive format detection

`TarFormat::from_file_name(filename)` works on a filename. I need to detect from a path. The filename is `file.file_name()`. I'll use that.

But what if `TarFormat::from_file_name` returns Raw for some real archive? E.g., if the asset URL doesn't have a recognizable extension. Then we don't do per-file verification. The fallback returns NoAttestations. That's safe — caller falls through to SLSA.

### Extraction

I'll use `file::untar` with `TarOptions::new(format)` and `strip_components: 0` to extract everything. The extraction location is a temp dir.

### Walking files

Walk recursively. Skip directories. Skip symlinks (they aren't regular files). Skip special files. For each regular file, compute path relative to extraction root for error messages.

### Performance considerations

Verifying each file makes N API calls. That could be slow for archives with many files. But:
1. This is the fallback path, only triggered when archive-level verification fails.
2. Most archives have a handful of files.
3. We can't batch the API calls because the underlying sigstore crate doesn't support it.

Acceptable.

### Error reporting

The error message should clearly state:
- Which file(s) failed
- The reason (no attestation, verification failed, etc.)
- The total count vs verified count

### Now let me code it up.

Let me also think about whether to add tests at the sigstore.rs level. The challenge: testing this requires either:
1. Mock attestation API responses (we'd need to mock the GitHub API)
2. Real API calls (network-dependent, flaky)
3. Test only the helper logic (file enumeration, policy decision) without actual verification

Option 3 is what I'll do. I'll structure the code so the decision logic (given a list of (path, result) tuples, decide the overall result) is testable separately. The extraction logic can be tested with real archives. The full end-to-end can be left to integration tests (which we won't add since they need network).

Let me design the code structure:

```rust
/// Result of verifying a single file inside an archive.
enum InnerFileOutcome {
    /// File had an attestation and it verified.
    Verified,
    /// File had an attestation but verification failed. Hard error.
    Failed(AttestationError),
    /// File had no attestation.
    NoAttestation,
}

/// Aggregate per-file outcomes into a final result.
fn aggregate_inner_outcomes(outcomes: Vec<(PathBuf, InnerFileOutcome)>) -> AttestationResult<bool> {
    if outcomes.is_empty() {
        return Err(AttestationError::Verification("archive contains no regular files".into()));
    }
    let all_verified = outcomes.iter().all(|(_, o)| matches!(o, InnerFileOutcome::Verified));
    if all_verified {
        return Ok(true);
    }
    let all_no_attestation = outcomes.iter().all(|(_, o)| matches!(o, InnerFileOutcome::NoAttestation));
    if all_no_attestation {
        return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
    }
    // Mixed: some verified, some failed, some no_attestation. Build a detailed error.
    let mut unverified = Vec::new();
    for (path, outcome) in &outcomes {
        match outcome {
            InnerFileOutcome::Verified => {},
            InnerFileOutcome::NoAttestation => {
                unverified.push(format!("{}: no attestation", path.display()));
            },
            InnerFileOutcome::Failed(e) => {
                unverified.push(format!("{}: {e}", path.display()));
            },
        }
    }
    Err(AttestationError::Verification(format!(
        "per-file attestation verification failed for {} of {} file(s) inside archive: {}",
        unverified.len(),
        outcomes.len(),
        unverified.join("; ")
    )))
}
```

Hmm wait. There's a question about how this aggregate relates to the hard-error-vs-soft-error distinction in callers.

Currently callers do:
- Ok(true) → success
- Ok(false) → hard error
- Err(NoAttestations) → fall through to SLSA
- Err(other) → hard error

With my design:
- All files verified → Ok(true) → success
- All files NoAttestations → Err(NoAttestations) → fall through to SLSA
- Anything else → Err(Verification) → hard error

That's the correct fail-closed behavior. 

But wait — there's a subtle issue. The existing `verify_attestation` returns `Ok(false)` if attestation exists but verification fails. But looking at the lib.rs code I read earlier:

```rust
pub async fn verify_github_attestation_inner(...) -> Result<bool> {
    ...
    let attestations = client.fetch_attestations(...).await?;
    if attestations.is_empty() {
        return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
    }
    ...
    verify_attestation_bundles(&attestations, &artifact, signer_workflow, &mut trust_roots).await
}
```

And `verify_attestation_bundles` returns `Ok(true)` on first bundle that verifies, or `Err(Verification(...))` if none verify. So actually `verify_attestation` never returns `Ok(false)` — it returns either `Ok(true)` or `Err(Verification(...))` or `Err(NoAttestations)` or other `Err`.

OK so the `Ok(false)` case in callers is dead code / defensive. I'll still preserve it in my logic to not break the caller contract.

Let me now think about how to structure the code.

### File structure

In `src/github/sigstore.rs`:
- New public function `verify_attestation_with_archive_fallback(...)` 
- Private helper `verify_each_archive_member(...)` 
- Private helper `aggregate_inner_outcomes(...)` — testable
- Private helper `list_archive_regular_files(archive_path, format) -> Result<Vec<PathBuf>>` — extracts to temp and lists

Actually, I realize I should also think about what to do about `Ok(false)`. Looking at the lib.rs:

```rust
async fn verify_github_attestation_inner(...) -> Result<bool> {
    ...
    if attestations.is_empty() {
        return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
    }
    ...
    verify_attestation_bundles(&attestations, &artifact, signer_workflow, &mut trust_roots).await
}
```

So for my fallback logic: when archive-level returns Ok(false), I'll propagate Ok(false) (don't fall back). When it returns Err(NoAttestations), I'll fall back. When it returns Ok(true), I'll return Ok(true). When it returns Err(other), I'll propagate.

OK let me also think about a subtle issue: the fallback uses `verify_attestation` recursively on each inner file. But `verify_attestation` itself returns Ok(true) or Err. It can't recurse infinitely because inner files aren't archives (we don't trigger fallback from within fallback). Wait, no — I'm calling `verify_attestation` (the original, archive-level-only function), NOT `verify_attestation_with_archive_fallback`. So no recursion. Good.

Let me also check: should the fallback re-verify the archive blob first? No — the caller already verified it returned NoAttestations. We go straight to per-file verification.

OK let me also think about how this integrates with the call sites. The call sites currently call `verify_attestation(...)`. I need to either:
(a) Replace those calls with `verify_attestation_with_archive_fallback(...)`, OR
(b) Make `verify_attestation` itself do the fallback.

Option (b) is cleaner — the fallback becomes a transparent feature of the existing function. But it changes behavior for ALL callers, including the lock-time and aqua paths. Is that desired? Let me think.

The task says: "Make mise accept those safely." So yes, all paths should benefit. Option (b) is correct.

But there's a subtlety: option (b) means the lock-time path would also do per-file verification when the archive is unattested. That seems right — we want lockfiles to record accurate provenance.

```

OK I'll go with option (b): make `verify_attestation` do the fallback transparently.

Wait, but I want to preserve existing archive-level verification as a distinct concept for testing and clarity. Let me think.

Actually, let me re-read the task: "preserve existing archive-level verification". This means I shouldn't *remove* the archive-level check. It doesn't mean I have to expose it as a separate function. The cleanest design preserves it as the first step within `verify_attestation`.

OK, decision: I'll modify `verify_attestation` to:

2. If that returns NoAttestations, try the per-file fallback.

Option (b) disadvantages:
- Behavior change for all callers (even ones we didn't think about).
- Could affect test fixtures that expected NoAttestations for a tarball.

Hmm. I think the better engineering choice is option (b) — make the fallback transparent. The chokepoint pattern is already established by this module's docstring: "Every call mise makes into `mise_sigstore` goes through this module." Adding the fallback at the chokepoint means it's always applied. And since the fallback fails closed, it can't make things less safe.

But wait, there's a subtle issue: the fallback extracts the archive to a temp dir. If the file isn't an archive, we skip extraction. If the file IS an archive but has attestations, we return Ok(true) without extracting. Good — extraction only happens on the NoAttestations branch.

OK let me go with option (b). 

But there's still a concern: what about the aqua backend? Looking at it:

```rust
async fn run_github_attestation_check(&self, artifact_path: &Path, pkg: &AquaPackage) -> Result<...> {
    ...
    match crate::github::sigstore::verify_attestation(artifact_path, ...).await {
        Ok(true) => Ok(Verified),
        Ok(false) => Err(...),
        Err(NoAttestations) => Ok(Unavailable),
        Err(e) => Err(...),
    }
}
```

If verify_attestation now does fallback, then on the NoAttestations path:
- If file is an archive and per-file fallback succeeds → returns Ok(true) → caller records Verified. Correct.
- If file is an archive and per-file fallback also returns NoAttestations (no inner attestations either) → returns Err(NoAttestations) → caller records Unavailable. Correct.
- If file is an archive and per-file fallback hard-fails → returns Err(Verification) → caller returns Err. Correct (fail closed).
- If file is not an archive → per-file fallback returns Err(NoAttestations) → caller records Unavailable. Correct.

Great, all call sites Just Work.

Let me now think about the lock-time path. It's similar — handles NoAttestations by falling through to SLSA. Same logic applies.

OK option (b) it is. Let me code.

### Implementation

First, the testable helper for archive member enumeration and policy aggregation:

```rust
/// Outcome of verifying a single regular file inside an archive.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
enum ArchiveMemberOutcome {
    /// The file had an attestation and it cryptographically verified.
    Verified,
    /// The file had an attestation but verification failed.
    Failed(String),
    /// The file had no attestation.
    NoAttestation,
}

/// Aggregate per-member verification outcomes into an overall result.
///
/// Policy: every regular file must verify. A single unverified file fails the
/// whole archive closed. The only "soft" failure mode is when no member has
/// any attestation at all — in that case we return `NoAttestations` so callers
/// can fall through to other verification mechanisms (SLSA). Any mixed state
/// (some verified, some not) is a hard error: it either indicates the
/// publisher forgot to attest something, or an attacker is smuggling an
/// unattested file alongside attested ones.
fn aggregate_archive_member_outcomes(
    outcomes: Vec<(String, ArchiveMemberOutcome)>,
) -> AttestationResult<bool> {
    if outcomes.is_empty() {
        return Err(AttestationError::Verification(
            "archive fallback found no regular files to verify".to_string(),
        ));
    }
    let total = outcomes.len();
    if outcomes
        .iter()
        .all(|(_, o)| matches!(o, ArchiveMemberOutcome::Verified))
    {
        return Ok(true);
    }
    if outcomes
        .iter()
        .all(|(_, o)| matches!(o, ArchiveMemberOutcome::NoAttestation))
    {
        return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
    }
    // Mixed: build a detailed error.
    let mut failures: Vec<String> = Vec::new();
    for (name, outcome) in &outcomes {
        match outcome {
            ArchiveMemberOutcome::Verified => {}
            ArchiveMemberOutcome::NoAttestation => {
                failures.push(format!("{name}: no GitHub attestation"));
            }
            ArchiveMemberOutcome::Failed(msg) => {
                failures.push(format!("{name}: {msg}"));
            }
        }
    }
    Err(AttestationError::Verification(format!(
        "per-file attestation verification failed for {}/{} archive member(s); \
         every regular file inside the archive must have a valid GitHub attestation: {}",
        failures.len(),
        total,
        failures.join("; ")
    )))
}
```

Then the archive member enumeration:

```rust
/// Recursively list every regular file under `root`, returning paths relative
/// to `root`. Symlinks, directories, and special files are skipped — only
/// regular files can carry attestations.
fn list_regular_files_recursive(root: &Path) -> Result<Vec<(PathBuf, PathBuf)>, std::io::Error> {
    let mut out = Vec::new();
    walk(root, root, &mut out)?;
    Ok(out)
}

fn walk(root: &Path, dir: &Path, out: &mut Vec<(PathBuf, PathBuf)>) -> std::io::Error {
    for entry in std::fs::read_dir(dir)? {
        let entry = entry?;
        let path = entry.path();
        let metadata = entry.metadata()?;  // follows symlinks
        // Actually we want to NOT follow symlinks. Use symlink_metadata.
        ...
    }
}
```

Hmm, let me think. For attestation verification we want to verify the actual bytes of each file. Symlinks could point outside the archive or to other files in the archive. We should skip symlinks entirely — only verify regular files.

Use `std::fs::symlink_metadata` to check the file type without following symlinks. If it's a regular file (not a symlink), include it. If it's a directory, recurse. If it's a symlink or special, skip.

Actually wait — I need to be careful. After extraction, the files in the temp dir might have been created by the extraction. We're checking the file types as they exist on disk. Using symlink_metadata we can tell symlinks apart from regular files.

Let me write the walk:

```rust
fn list_regular_files(root: &Path) -> std::io::Result<Vec<PathBuf>> {
    let mut out = Vec::new();
    let mut stack = vec![root.to_path_buf()];
    while let Some(dir) = stack.pop() {
        for entry in std::fs::read_dir(&dir)? {
            let entry = entry?;
            let path = entry.path();
            // symlink_metadata so we don't follow symlinks
            let metadata = std::fs::symlink_metadata(&path)?;
            if metadata.is_dir() {
                stack.push(path);
            } else if metadata.is_file() {
                out.push(path);
            }
            // else: symlink, fifo, socket, etc. — skip
        }
    }
    Ok(out)
}
```

Then the per-file verification:

```rust
async fn verify_attestation_via_archive_contents(
    archive_path: &Path,
    owner: &str,
    repo: &str,
    expected_workflow: Option<&str>,
    api_url: Option<&str>,
) -> AttestationResult<bool> {
    // Detect archive format. If not an archive, signal no attestation found
    // so callers can fall through to other mechanisms.
    let format = archive_format(archive_path);
    let Some(format) = format else {
        return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
    };
    // Extract to temp dir
    let tmp = tempfile_tempdir()?;  // need tempfile crate
    let opts = crate::file::TarOptions::new(format);  // strip_components: 0, preserve_mtime: doesn't matter
    crate::file::untar(archive_path, tmp.path(), &opts)?;  // converts io::Error to AttestationError::Io
    
    // List regular files
    let members = list_regular_files(tmp.path()).map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
    
    let mut outcomes = Vec::with_capacity(members.len());
    for member in members {
        let display = member.strip_prefix(tmp.path()).unwrap_or(&member).display().to_string();
        let result = verify_attestation_blob(&member, owner, repo, expected_workflow, api_url).await;
        let outcome = match result {
            Ok(true) => ArchiveMemberOutcome::Verified,
            Ok(false) => ArchiveMemberOutcome::Failed("attestation verification returned false".to_string()),
            Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations) => ArchiveMemberOutcome::NoAttestation,
            Err(e) => ArchiveMemberOutcome::Failed(e.to_string()),
        };
        outcomes.push((display, outcome));
    }
    
    aggregate_archive_member_outcomes(outcomes)
}
```

Hmm, but there's an issue: I need to call the *original* (archive-level-only) verify_attestation on each inner file, not the new fallback-aware one (else infinite recursion). Let me factor out the archive-level logic into a private `verify_attestation_blob`.

Actually let me re-examine the existing code structure. The existing `verify_attestation` directly calls `mise_sigstore::verify_github_attestation[_with_base_url]`. So I can:

1. Rename current `verify_attestation` to `verify_attestation_blob` (private).
2. Add new `verify_attestation` (public) that:
   - Calls `verify_attestation_blob`
   - On NoAttestations, calls `verify_attestation_via_archive_contents`
   - Otherwise propagates

Wait, but the existing function is in `src/github/sigstore.rs` and calls into the `mise_sigstore` crate. Let me rename it.

Actually let me look at the function names again. In `src/github/sigstore.rs`:
- `verify_attestation` (public)

I'll rename this to `verify_attestation_blob` (private) and add a new public `verify_attestation` that does the fallback orchestration.

But wait — should I keep the new function as a separate name to make the archive-level path still callable explicitly? Hmm, no callers need that. They all want the fallback behavior. Let me just make `verify_attestation` do the fallback.

Actually, for clarity and for tests, let me have:
- `verify_attestation` (public) — does fallback
- `verify_attestation_blob` (private) — archive-level only (renamed from old `verify_attestation`)

OK now let me think about edge cases:

1. **Path that doesn't have a recognizable extension**: `from_file_name` returns `Raw`. We should treat Raw as "not an archive". Don't do fallback. Return NoAttestations.

2. **Zip/7z extraction**: mise's `untar` handles Zip and 7z. But 7z is only supported on Windows (`#[cfg(windows)]`). On non-Windows, 7z returns an error from `untar`. We propagate.

3. **Temp dir cleanup**: Use `tempfile::TempDir` which auto-cleans on drop. The mise crate already uses tempfile (I saw it in github.rs).

4. **Symlink loops**: If the archive contains a symlink loop (rare), `list_regular_files` could loop forever. Use symlink_metadata to avoid following symlinks. Good.

5. **Hidden files**: Should we skip dotfiles? No — the policy is every regular file. Hidden files must verify too.

6. **Empty files**: An empty file (0 bytes) has a known digest (e3b0c44...). It might or might not have an attestation. Treat it like any other file.

7. **Single-file archive**: E.g., `binary.gz` is a single-file gzip. But `is_archive()` returns false for `Gz`. So we don't do fallback. Good.

8. **tar.gz containing one tar containing binaries**: We'd extract the outer tar.gz, then verify the inner tar as a regular file. The publisher's attestation is over the inner tar's bytes. That works.

Now, what about the test setup. Let me think about what tests I can add:

1. **`aggregate_archive_member_outcomes` tests** (unit, no network):
   - All verified → Ok(true)
   - Empty → Err(Verification)
   - All no_attestation → Err(NoAttestations)
   - Mixed verified + no_attestation → Err(Verification)
   - Mixed verified + failed → Err(Verification)
   - All failed → Err(Verification)

2. **`list_regular_files` tests** (filesystem, no network):
   - Lists regular files in nested dirs
   - Skips symlinks
   - Skips directories, only files
   - Handles hidden files

3. **`archive_format` tests** (string parsing, no network):
   - .tar.gz → TarGz
   - .zip → Zip
   - .exe → None (not an archive)
   - no extension → None

4. **End-to-end test for the fallback** (needs network): Skip — can't reliably test against GitHub API.

5. **Integration test with a fake/no-attestation archive**: We can create a real .tar.gz with files inside, then verify the fallback correctly extracts and enumerates them. We can't easily test the actual attestation check without mocking. But we can test that:
   - For a non-archive file, fallback returns NoAttestations without extracting.
   - For an archive file, fallback extracts and enumerates correctly.

Let me think about how to test the "fallback correctly extracts and enumerates" part. I could create a real tar.gz with files inside, and verify that `list_regular_files` after extraction returns the expected paths. That's testable.

For the verify_attestation_via_archive_contents function itself, I'd need to mock the network. Let me see if mise has a test pattern for this... probably not. I'll focus on testing the helper functions and the policy aggregation.

Let me also think: should I add tests at the e2e level? E2E tests in this repo are bash scripts in `e2e/`. They need real tools. I won't add e2e tests for this — too brittle.

Let me look at how tempfile is used in mise tests.
Now I have a clear design. Let me check a few more things before implementing.

+/// per-file archive fallback be applied uniformly.
+///
+/// Verification proceeds in two stages:

+///    This is what most release pipelines produce and what mise has always done; it is
+///    preserved unchanged.
+/// 2. **Per-file archive fallback.** Only if stage 1 reports that *no* attestation

+///    artifact is a supported archive format. The archive is extracted to a temporary
+///    directory and each regular file inside is verified individually via
+///    [`verify_attestation_blob`]. Every regular file must verify, otherwise the
+///    archive is rejected. See [`aggregate_archive_member_outcomes`] for the policy.
+///
+/// Stage 2 is **fail-closed**: a single unattested inner file fails the whole archive,
+/// and any error other than `NoAttestations` from stage 1 (network failure, malformed
+/// bundle, cryptographic mismatch) is propagated without attempting a fallback —
+/// retrying after a real failure would be guessing toward permissiveness.

/// Extract the archive at `archive_path` to a fresh temp dir and return
/// `(temp_dir, members)` where members are absolute paths to regular files inside.
/// The temp_dir is returned so the caller can keep it alive for the duration of
/// verification.
fn extract_archive_members(archive_path: &Path) -> AttestationResult<(tempfile::TempDir, Vec<PathBuf>)> {
    let Some(format) = archive_format_for_path(archive_path) else {
        return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
    };
    let tmp = tempfile::tempdir().map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
    let opts = TarOptions { strip_components: 0, ..TarOptions::new(format) };
    crate::file::untar(archive_path, tmp.path(), &opts).map_err(|e| ...)?;
    let members = list_regular_files(tmp.path()).map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
    Ok((tmp, members))
}
```

Then `verify_attestation_via_archive_contents` calls this and does the rest. Tests can call `extract_archive_members` and verify the extracted files.

The tests I'll add:

### 1. Policy aggregation tests (`aggregate_archive_member_outcomes`)

- `aggregate_empty_returns_verification_error`

### 2. Result mapping tests (`result_to_outcome`)

### 3. Format detection tests (`archive_format_for_path`)
- `archive_format_detects_tar_gz_variants`

### 4. File listing tests (`list_regular_files`)
- `list_regular_files_returns_empty_for_empty_dir`
- `list_regular_files_finds_nested_regular_files`
- `list_regular_files_skips_directories`
- `list_regular_files_includes_hidden_files` (strict-by-design)
- `list_regular_files_returns_sorted_paths`
- `list_regular_files_skips_symlinks_to_files` (Unix only)
- `list_regular_files_skips_symlinks_to_dirs_and_does_not_recurse_them` (Unix only)
- `list_regular_files_does_not_follow_symlink_loop` (Unix only)

### 5. End-to-end extraction tests (`extract_archive_members`)
- `extract_archive_members_returns_not_an_archive_for_binary`

- `extract_archive_members_extracts_zip_and_lists_members`

- `extract_archive_members_errors_on_corrupt_archive`

### 6. relative_display_name tests
- `relative_display_name_strips_root_prefix`
- `relative_display_name_falls_back_when_not_under_root`

Let me write them all.

Actually let me focus on what's most important and not add too many redundant tests. The most important things to test:

1. The **policy** (`aggregate_archive_member_outcomes`) — this is the security boundary. Pin every case.
2. The **result mapping** (`result_to_outcome`) — the API result → outcome translation. If this is wrong, NoAttestations could be mis-mapped to Failed or vice versa.
3. The **file enumeration** (`list_regular_files`) — especially symlink safety.
4. The **format detection** (`archive_format_for_path`).
5. End-to-end **extraction** (`extract_archive_members`).

Let me write these.

- Build tar.gz archives in memory using `tar` + `flate2` crates.
- Build zip archives using `zip` crate.
- Create files/dirs/symlinks using std::fs.

Let me look at how mise uses tar/flate2 internally to make sure my approach matches.

state.title: src/github/sigstore.rs

part.text:
The unused `std::io::Write` import in `build_tar_gz` is because tar's `append_data` takes a `Read`, not `Write`. The `Write` trait is needed for the GzEncoder's `.finish()` to actually be a Writer... actually let me look. `GzEncoder::new(file, ...)` returns a `GzEncoder<W> where W: Write`. The `file` is `File` which impls `Write`. The `.finish()` method comes from the inner writer. Hmm.

Actually, `GzEncoder` itself doesn't need Write to be imported to call `.finish()` — the trait bound is on the type, not the call site. So `Write` import is genuinely unused.

Let me remove it.

part.text:
Almost all tests pass. One failure on line 1223 — let me look. The test asserts:

```rust
let tool_bytes = std::fs::read(extracted_root.join("bin/tool")).unwrap();
```

This panics with NotFound. That's because `extracted_root` is moved out when I get the path. Let me re-read my test code.

Actually wait — `tmp` is the TempDir. I extracted `tmp.path().to_path_buf()` to a variable, then... oh wait, `tmp` is consumed by the match arm. Let me check.

```rust
let (extracted_root, members) = match extract_archive_members(&archive).unwrap() {
    ExtractResult::Extracted { tmp, members } => (tmp.path().to_path_buf(), members),
    ExtractResult::NotAnArchive => panic!("tar.gz must extract"),
};
```

I get `tmp` from destructuring. Then `tmp.path().to_path_buf()` clones the path. But `tmp` then goes out of scope at the end of the match arm and is dropped — and the drop removes the temp directory! So when I try to read `bin/tool` after the match, the file is gone.

I need to keep `tmp` alive outside the match. Let me restructure.
The `tmp` is being dropped at the end of the match arm. Let me fix the test:

- `mise run lint-fix` - Run linting and automatically fix issues
- `mise run format` - Format code (part of CI task)
- `mise run ci` - Run format, build, and test

The lints likely include cargo fmt, clippy. Let me run those directly.

part.text:
Both fmt and clippy are clean. Let me also verify the diff once more to make sure I haven't missed anything.

Let me also think about whether I need to update the documentation. The task says "preserve existing archive-level verification". The user-facing behavior changes:
- Archives whose inner files are attested (but not the archive blob) now verify successfully.
- Archives that mix attested and unattested files now hard-fail.

The docstring for `verify_attestation` already documents this new behavior. Should I update any external docs? Let me check if there's a relevant doc file.
Let me check if there are docs I should update:

state.output:
Found 12 matches
/app/docs/tips-and-tricks.md:
  Line 151: mise provides **native software verification** for aqua tools without requiring external dependencies. For aqua tools, Cosign/Minisign signatures, SLSA provenance, and GitHub artifact attestations are verified automatically using mise's built-in implementation.


/app/docs/paranoid.md:
  Line 67: GitHub artifact attestations) at install time, even when the lockfile already has a


/app/docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md:
  Line 326: When `mise lock` generates a lockfile, it records a verified provenance type (e.g., `slsa`, `cosign`, `minisign`, `github-attestations`) for each tool when one is available. For the **current platform**, mise downloads the artifact and performs full cryptographic verification at lock time -- ensuring the provenance entry in the lockfile is backed by actual verification, not just registry metadata. This applies to both the aqua and github backends. For cross-platform entries, provenance is detected from registry metadata without verification (since the artifact may not be runnable on the current machine).

  Line 330: If GitHub Artifact Attestations are enabled but the GitHub API confirms none exist for a checksum-backed artifact, mise may record `github_attestations = "unavailable"`. This is a negative cache entry, not provenance: it only skips the redundant GitHub attestation probe on later installs from that lockfile. Other verification paths such as SLSA, Cosign, Minisign, and checksum verification still run as usual.


/app/docs/dev-tools/backends/aqua.md:
  Line 115: # Enable/disable GitHub artifact attestations verification (default: true)

  Line 175: ✓ GitHub artifact attestations verified


/app/docs/dev-tools/backends/vfox.md:
  Line 13: - **Security** — [tool plugins](../../tool-plugin-development.md) support attestation verification (GitHub artifact attestations, cosign signatures, SLSA provenance) for downloaded artifacts. When a tool plugin's `PreInstall` hook returns an `attestation` table, mise verifies it during install and records the result in `mise.lock`, protecting against downgrade attacks on subsequent installs. Backend plugins do not currently support attestation


/app/CHANGELOG.md:
  Line 203: - **(lock)** cache unavailable github attestations by @risu729 in [#9741](https://github.com/jdx/mise/pull/9741)

  Line 948: - **(github)** route artifact attestation verification to custom api_url by @jdx in [#9254](https://github.com/jdx/mise/pull/9254)

  Line 1463: - **(install)** add locked_verify_provenance setting and detect github attestations at lock time by @jdx in [#8901](https://github.com/jdx/mise/pull/8901)

  Line 2621: - **(settings)** remove wrong tip for github_attestations by @risu729 in [#8158](https://github.com/jdx/mise/pull/8158)

<path>/app/docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
320: # Set versions based on package.json
321: mise use node@$(jq -r '.engines.node' package.json)
322: ```
323: 
324: ## Provenance and Security
325: 
326: When `mise lock` generates a lockfile, it records a verified provenance type (e.g., `slsa`, `cosign`, `minisign`, `github-attestations`) for each tool when one is available. For the **current platform**, mise downloads the artifact and performs full cryptographic verification at lock time -- ensuring the provenance entry in the lockfile is backed by actual verification, not just registry metadata. This applies to both the aqua and github backends. For cross-platform entries, provenance is detected from registry metadata without verification (since the artifact may not be runnable on the current machine).
327: 
328: By default, when `mise install` sees a lockfile with both a checksum and a verified provenance entry, it trusts the lockfile and skips re-verification. This avoids redundant API calls (e.g., GitHub attestation queries) which can cause rate limit issues in CI. Since the current platform's provenance was already verified during `mise lock`, this is safe.
329: 
330: If GitHub Artifact Attestations are enabled but the GitHub API confirms none exist for a checksum-backed artifact, mise may record `github_attestations = "unavailable"`. This is a negative cache entry, not provenance: it only skips the redundant GitHub attestation probe on later installs from that lockfile. Other verification paths such as SLSA, Cosign, Minisign, and checksum verification still run as usual.
331: 
332: GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. A later `mise lock` run or `MISE_LOCKED_VERIFY_PROVENANCE=1 mise install` can discover attestations added after the lockfile recorded them as unavailable.

metadata.preview:
# Set versions based on package.json
mise use node@$(jq -r '.engines.node' package.json)
```

## Provenance and Security

When `mise lock` generates a lockfile, it records a verified provenance type (e.g., `slsa`, `cosign`, `minisign`, `github-attestations`) for each tool when one is available. For the **current platform**, mise downloads the artifact and performs full cryptographic verification at lock time -- ensuring the provenance entry in the lockfile is backed by actual verification, not just registry metadata. This applies to both the aqua and github backends. For cross-platform entries, provenance is detected from registry metadata without verification (since the artifact may not be runnable on the current machine).

By default, when `mise install` sees a lockfile with both a checksum and a verified provenance entry, it trusts the lockfile and skips re-verification. This avoids redundant API calls (e.g., GitHub attestation queries) which can cause rate limit issues in CI. Since the current platform's provenance was already verified during `mise lock`, this is safe.

If GitHub Artifact Attestations are enabled but the GitHub API confirms none exist for a checksum-backed artifact, mise may record `github_attestations = "unavailable"`. This is a negative cache entry, not provenance: it only skips the redundant GitHub attestation probe on later installs from that lockfile. Other verification paths such as SLSA, Cosign, Minisign, and checksum verification still run as usual.

GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. A later `mise lock` run or `MISE_LOCKED_VERIFY_PROVENANCE=1 mise install` can discover attestations added after the lockfile recorded them as unavailable.

For additional security, you can force provenance re-verification at install time on every install:

display.text:
# Set versions based on package.json
mise use node@$(jq -r '.engines.node' package.json)
```

## Provenance and Security

When `mise lock` generates a lockfile, it records a verified provenance type (e.g., `slsa`, `cosign`, `minisign`, `github-attestations`) for each tool when one is available. For the **current platform**, mise downloads the artifact and performs full cryptographic verification at lock time -- ensuring the provenance entry in the lockfile is backed by actual verification, not just registry metadata. This applies to both the aqua and github backends. For cross-platform entries, provenance is detected from registry metadata without verification (since the artifact may not be runnable on the current machine).

By default, when `mise install` sees a lockfile with both a checksum and a verified provenance entry, it trusts the lockfile and skips re-verification. This avoids redundant API calls (e.g., GitHub attestation queries) which can cause rate limit issues in CI. Since the current platform's provenance was already verified during `mise lock`, this is safe.

If GitHub Artifact Attestations are enabled but the GitHub API confirms none exist for a checksum-backed artifact, mise may record `github_attestations = "unavailable"`. This is a negative cache entry, not provenance: it only skips the redundant GitHub attestation probe on later installs from that lockfile. Other verification paths such as SLSA, Cosign, Minisign, and checksum verification still run as usual.

GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. <content>
1: # Security Policy
2: 
3: mise is a convenient tool to manage developer tools, however its model is also open to potential risks. The following
4: are major areas of mise and the security considerations currently being made and what needs to be made in the future.
5: 
6: Please open a ticket or send me an email if you have thoughts on how mise can be made more secure.
7: 
8: ## Core CLI Security
9: 
10: Development of the "core CLI" is done on jdx/mise which only a single developer (me, @jdx) has access to.
11: Other contributors may only submit contributions via public Pull Requests. Reducing the number
12: of developers with access down to 1 minimizes the chance of keys being leaked.
13: 
14: This does create a [bus factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) problem. If I suddenly died one day
15: or otherwise wasn't able to continue development at all there are some successors listed in my GitHub account
16: that can take over my account if need be.
17: 
18: The dependencies in the core CLI are a security vector. I've tried to be judicious about what dependencies make it into
19: the project. I only select dependencies with broad usage across the Rust community where possible.
20: I'm open to PRs or suggestions on reducing dependency count even at the cost of functionality because it will make
21: mise more secure.
22: 
23: ## mise.en.dev
24: 
25: mise.en.dev is the asset host for mise. It's used to host precompiled mise CLI binaries, and hosts a "[VERSION](https://mise.en.dev/VERSION)"
26: which mise uses to occasionally check for a new version being released. Everything hosted there uses a single
27: vendor to reduce surface area.
28: 
29: ## Native Security Verification
30: 
31: mise provides **native Rust implementation** for security verification of tools, eliminating the need for external dependencies like `cosign`, `slsa-verifier`, `minisign`, or `gh` CLI tools. This applies to tools using the aqua backend.
32: 
33: ### Supported Verification Methods
34: 
35: - **Cosign signatures**: Keyless and key-based signature verification
36: - **SLSA provenance**: Verification of Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA) attestations
37: - **GitHub Artifact Attestations**: Verification of GitHub's artifact attestation system
38: - **Minisign verification**: Verification of minisign signatures
39: - **Checksum verification**: Always enabled for supported backends
40: 
41: ### Configuration
42: 
43: All verification methods are enabled by default and can be configured via environment variables:
44: 
45: ```bash
46: # Enable/disable specific verification methods
47: export MISE_AQUA_COSIGN=true                 # Default: true
48: export MISE_AQUA_SLSA=true                   # Default: true
49: export MISE_AQUA_GITHUB_ATTESTATIONS=true    # Default: true
50: export MISE_AQUA_MINISIGN=true               # Default: true
51: ```
52: 
53: ### How it Works
54: 
55: You will see this verification happen automatically when aqua tools are installed. The verification status is displayed during installation with progress indicators. If any verification fails, the installation will be aborted.
56: 
57: See the [aqua docs](https://aquaproj.github.io/docs/reference/security/cosign-slsa) for more on how verification is configured in the [aqua registry](https://github.com/aquaproj/aqua-registry).
58: 
59: If you notice a tool offers security verification methods (gpg/slsa/cosign/minisign/etc), consider making a PR to the aqua registry to enable verification for that tool.
60: 
61: ## `mise.lock`
62: 
63: mise has support for [lockfiles](https://mise.en.dev/configuration/settings.html#lockfile) which will
64: store/verify the checksum of tool tarballs. Committing this into your repository is a good way to ensure
65: that the exact same version of a tool is installed across all developers and CI/CD systems.
66: 
67: Not all backends support this—notably asdf plugins do not.
68: 
69: ## asdf plugins
70: 
71: asdf plugins in asdf (but not with mise's default tools) are dangerous. They are typically owned by random developers
72: unconnected to either asdf or the tool vendor. They may get hacked or maliciously inject code into
73: their plugin that could trivially execute code on your machine.
74: 
75: asdf plugins are not used for tools inside the [registry](https://github.com/jdx/mise/blob/main/registry/) whenever possible.
76: Sometimes it is not possible to use more secure backends like aqua/ubi because tools have complex install
77: setups or need to export env vars. As of 2025-01-08, <25% of tools use asdf plugins as the default backend.
78: All of these are hosted in the [mise-plugins org](https://github.com/mise-plugins) to secure the supply
79: chain so you do not need to rely on plugins maintained by anyone except me.
80: 
81: Of course if you _manually_ add plugins not from the mise-plugins org you will want to ensure they
82: are coming from a trusted source.
83: 
84: Please contribute to this effort to migrate away from asdf plugins by checking if a tool works in ubi or aqua and submitting a PR to
85: [registry/](https://github.com/jdx/mise/blob/main/registry/) to add it. If it doesn't work
86: in ubi or is missing from aqua, submit an issue or PR to the respective project to add it. New tools
87: using asdf are **not** likely to be accepted unless they cannot be supported with any other backend.
88: 
89: ## Supported Versions
90: 
91: The only supported version is the most recent one.
92: 
93: ## Reporting a Vulnerability
94: 
95: Send an email to security@<MISE_JDX_DOMAIN_IN_README>
96: 
97: If you want, you may encrypt the message with GPG:

# Security Policy

mise is a convenient tool to manage developer tools, however its model is also open to potential risks. The following
are major areas of mise and the security considerations currently being made and what needs to be made in the future.

Please open a ticket or send me an email if you have thoughts on how mise can be made more secure.

## Core CLI Security

Development of the "core CLI" is done on jdx/mise which only a single developer (me, @jdx) has access to.
Other contributors may only submit contributions via public Pull Requests. Reducing the number
of developers with access down to 1 minimizes the chance of keys being leaked.

This does create a [bus factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) problem. If I suddenly died one day
or otherwise wasn't able to continue development at all there are some successors listed in my GitHub account
that can take over my account if need be.

The dependencies in the core CLI are a security vector. I've tried to be judicious about what dependencies make it into
the project. I only select dependencies with broad usage across the Rust community where possible.
I'm open to PRs or suggestions on reducing dependency count even at the cost of functionality because it will make

display.text:
# Security Policy

mise is a convenient tool to manage developer tools, however its model is also open to potential risks. The following
are major areas of mise and the security considerations currently being made and what needs to be made in the future.

Please open a ticket or send me an email if you have thoughts on how mise can be made more secure.

## Core CLI Security

Development of the "core CLI" is done on jdx/mise which only a single developer (me, @jdx) has access to.
Other contributors may only submit contributions via public Pull Requests. Reducing the number
of developers with access down to 1 minimizes the chance of keys being leaked.

This does create a [bus factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) problem. If I suddenly died one day
or otherwise wasn't able to continue development at all there are some successors listed in my GitHub account
that can take over my account if need be.

The dependencies in the core CLI are a security vector. I've tried to be judicious about what dependencies make it into
the project. I only select dependencies with broad usage across the Rust community where possible.
I'm open to PRs or suggestions on reducing dependency count even at the cost of functionality because it will make
mise more secure.

## mise.en.dev

mise.en.dev is the asset host for mise. It's used to host precompiled mise CLI binaries, and hosts a "[VERSION](https://mise.en.dev/VERSION)"
which mise uses to occasionally check for a new version being released. Everything hosted there uses a single
vendor to reduce surface area.

## Native Security Verification

mise provides **native Rust implementation** for security verification of tools, eliminating the need for external dependencies like `cosign`, `slsa-verifier`, `minisign`, or `gh` CLI tools. This applies to tools using the aqua backend.

### Supported Verification Methods

- **Cosign signatures**: Keyless and key-based signature verification
- **SLSA provenance**: Verification of Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA) attestations
- **GitHub Artifact Attestations**: Verification of GitHub's artifact attestation system
- **Minisign verification**: Verification of minisign signatures
- **Checksum verification**: Always enabled for supported backends

### Configuration

All verification methods are enabled by default and can be configured via environment variables:

```bash
# Enable/disable specific verification methods
export MISE_AQUA_COSIGN=true                 # Default: true
export MISE_AQUA_SLSA=true                   # Default: true
export MISE_AQUA_GITHUB_ATTESTATIONS=true    # Default: true
export MISE_AQUA_MINISIGN=true               # Default: true
```

### How it Works

You will see this verification happen automatically when aqua tools are installed. The verification status is displayed during installation with progress indicators. If any verification fails, the installation will be aborted.

See the [aqua docs](https://aquaproj.github.io/docs/reference/security/cosign-slsa) for more on how verification is configured in the [aqua registry](https://github.com/aquaproj/aqua-registry).

If you notice a tool offers security verification methods (gpg/slsa/cosign/minisign/etc), consider making a PR to the aqua registry to enable verification for that tool.

## `mise.lock`

mise has support for [lockfiles](https://mise.en.dev/configuration/settings.html#lockfile) which will
store/verify the checksum of tool tarballs. Committing this into your repository is a good way to ensure
that the exact same version of a tool is installed across all developers and CI/CD systems.

Not all backends support this—notably asdf plugins do not.

## asdf plugins

asdf plugins in asdf (but not with mise's default tools) are dangerous. They are typically owned by random developers
unconnected to either asdf or the tool vendor. They may get hacked or maliciously inject code into
their plugin that could trivially execute code on your machine.

asdf plugins are not used for tools inside the [registry](https://github.com/jdx/mise/blob/main/registry/) whenever possible.
Sometimes it is not possible to use more secure backends like aqua/ubi because tools have complex install
setups or need to export env vars. As of 2025-01-08, <25% of tools use asdf plugins as the default backend.
All of these are hosted in the [mise-plugins org](https://github.com/mise-plugins) to secure the supply
chain so you do not need to rely on plugins maintained by anyone except me.

Of course if you _manually_ add plugins not from the mise-plugins org you will want to ensure they
are coming from a trusted source.

Please contribute to this effort to migrate away from asdf plugins by checking if a tool works in ubi or aqua and submitting a PR to
[registry/](https://github.com/jdx/mise/blob/main/registry/) to add it. If it doesn't work
in ubi or is missing from aqua, submit an issue or PR to the respective project to add it. New tools
using asdf are **not** likely to be accepted unless they cannot be supported with any other backend.

## Supported Versions

The only supported version is the most recent one.

## Reporting a Vulnerability

Send an email to security@<MISE_JDX_DOMAIN_IN_README>

If you want, you may encrypt the message with GPG:

<details>
  <summary>@jdx's public key</summary>
