part.text:
GitHub backend docs don't mention attestations explicitly. The most relevant doc is `docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md` which already has a section on "Provenance and Security". Let me add a brief note there about the per-file fallback.

Actually, let me focus on getting the implementation right and committing it. The user didn't explicitly ask for docs. I'll add a brief, focused note to mise-lock.md since that's where this feature's behavior is documented. I'll keep it short.
Let me add a short, focused note in the most-relevant doc:

input.newString:
GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. A later `mise lock` run or `MISE_LOCKED_VERIFY_PROVENANCE=1 mise install` can discover attestations added after the lockfile recorded them as unavailable.

### Per-file archive attestation fallback

Some release pipelines sign each file *inside* a release archive rather than the archive blob itself. When GitHub artifact attestation verification is enabled and the archive's own digest has no attestation, mise transparently extracts the archive to a temporary directory and verifies each inner file's digest against the GitHub attestations API. The fallback is **fail-closed**: every regular file inside the archive must carry a valid attestation. A single unattested file (README, LICENSE, or anything else) rejects the whole archive. This is the only policy with no smuggling surface; an attacker who can substitute archive contents but not the signing key cannot slip an unattested file past verification. The fallback only triggers when the archive itself has no attestation — an archive that *does* have an attestation which fails cryptographically is rejected without retrying against inner files. Non-archive artifacts (raw binaries, single-file `.gz`/`.xz` compressions) are unaffected.

For additional security, you can force provenance re-verification at install time on every install:
input.oldString:
GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. A later `mise lock` run or `MISE_LOCKED_VERIFY_PROVENANCE=1 mise install` can discover attestations added after the lockfile recorded them as unavailable.

For additional security, you can force provenance re-verification at install time on every install:

+++ /app/docs/dev-tools/mise-lock.md
@@ -330,8 +330,12 @@
 If GitHub Artifact Attestations are enabled but the GitHub API confirms none exist for a checksum-backed artifact, mise may record `github_attestations = "unavailable"`. This is a negative cache entry, not provenance: it only skips the redundant GitHub attestation probe on later installs from that lockfile. Other verification paths such as SLSA, Cosign, Minisign, and checksum verification still run as usual.
 
 GitHub's docs show binary attestations generated from an existing artifact path with [`actions/attest`](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/secure-your-work/use-artifact-attestations/use-artifact-attestations#generating-build-provenance-for-binaries), and the REST API lists attestations by [subject digest](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/orgs/attestations#list-attestations). That means an attestation can appear after the release asset was uploaded. A later `mise lock` run or `MISE_LOCKED_VERIFY_PROVENANCE=1 mise install` can discover attestations added after the lockfile recorded them as unavailable.
 
+### Per-file archive attestation fallback
+
+Some release pipelines sign each file *inside* a release archive rather than the archive blob itself. When GitHub artifact attestation verification is enabled and the archive's own digest has no attestation, mise transparently extracts the archive to a temporary directory and verifies each inner file's digest against the GitHub attestations API. The fallback is **fail-closed**: every regular file inside the archive must carry a valid attestation. A single unattested file (README, LICENSE, or anything else) rejects the whole archive. This is the only policy with no smuggling surface; an attacker who can substitute archive contents but not the signing key cannot slip an unattested file past verification. The fallback only triggers when the archive itself has no attestation — an archive that *does* have an attestation which fails cryptographically is rejected without retrying against inner files. Non-archive artifacts (raw binaries, single-file `.gz`/`.xz` compressions) are unaffected.
+

+/// Archive-level (blob digest) verification against the GitHub attestations API.
+///
+/// This is the historical verification path: compute the SHA-256 of the file on disk,
+/// ask GitHub for attestations under that digest, and cryptographically verify any that
+/// come back. It has no knowledge of archive contents — the caller's `artifact_path` is
+/// treated as an opaque blob. The per-file archive fallback lives one level up in
+/// [`verify_attestation`].
+async fn verify_attestation_blob(
+    artifact_path: &Path,
+    owner: &str,
+    repo: &str,
+    expected_workflow: Option<&str>,
+    api_url: Option<&str>,
 ) -> AttestationResult<bool> {
     let token = resolve_token_for_wrapper(api_url);
     match api_url {
@@ -78,6 +155,286 @@ pub async fn verify_attestation(
     }
 }
 
+// =============================================================================
+// Per-file archive fallback
+// =============================================================================
+
+/// Outcome of running [`verify_attestation_blob`] against one regular file inside an
+/// extracted archive. Captured so [`aggregate_archive_member_outcomes`] can apply the
+/// "every member must verify" policy in a separately-testable pure function.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
+enum ArchiveMemberOutcome {
+    /// The file had at least one attestation and it cryptographically verified.
+    Verified,
+    /// The file had at least one attestation but verification failed (signature,
+    /// certificate chain, workflow mismatch, etc.). Hard failure.
+    Failed(String),
+    /// The GitHub API returned no attestations for this file's digest.
+    NoAttestation,
+}
+
+/// Verify a GitHub artifact attestation against each regular file inside an archive.
+///
+/// Triggered only by [`verify_attestation`] after archive-level verification reports
+/// [`AttestationError::NoAttestations`]. Extraction uses mise's standard
+/// [`crate::file::untar`] so the supported formats and decompression logic match the
+/// install path exactly.
+///
+/// Returns:
+/// - `Ok(true)` when every regular file inside the archive verifies.
+/// - [`AttestationError::NoAttestations`] when no inner file has any attestation at all
+///   (so callers can fall through to SLSA / checksum verification, exactly as they
+///   would have without the fallback).
+/// - A `Verification` error in every other case — including partial coverage, where
+///   some members verified but others did not. Partial coverage is the smuggling signal
+///   and must fail closed.
+/// - [`AttestationError::NoAttestations`] when `artifact_path` isn't a recognized
+///   archive format. The fallback simply doesn't apply.
+async fn verify_attestation_via_archive_contents(
+    artifact_path: &Path,
+    owner: &str,
+    repo: &str,
+    expected_workflow: Option<&str>,
+    api_url: Option<&str>,
+) -> AttestationResult<bool> {
+    let (tmp, members) = match extract_archive_members(artifact_path)? {
+        ExtractResult::NotAnArchive => {
+            debug!(
+                "attestation fallback skipped: {} is not a recognized archive format",
+                artifact_path.display()
+            );
+            // Not an archive — the fallback does not apply. Surface NoAttestations so the
+            // caller's existing fall-through (to SLSA, checksums, etc.) still works.
+            return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
+        }
+        ExtractResult::Extracted { tmp, members } => (tmp, members),
+    };
+
+    debug!(
+        "attestation fallback: verifying {} regular file(s) extracted from {}",
+        members.len(),
+        artifact_path.display()
+    );
+
+    let root = tmp.path();
+    let mut outcomes: Vec<(String, ArchiveMemberOutcome)> = Vec::with_capacity(members.len());
+    for member in &members {
+        let display = relative_display_name(member, root);
+        let result = verify_attestation_blob(member, owner, repo, expected_workflow, api_url).await;
+        outcomes.push((display, result_to_outcome(result)));
+    }
+
+    let result = aggregate_archive_member_outcomes(outcomes);
+    match &result {
+        Ok(true) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback succeeded: every member of {} verified",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+        Ok(false) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback reported verification false for {}",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+        Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback found no attestations for any member of {}",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+        Err(e) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback failed for {}: {e}",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+    }
+    result
+}
+
+/// Result of [`extract_archive_members`]: either a real archive was extracted (with the
+/// temp dir held alive for the caller), or the path wasn't an archive at all.
+#[derive(Debug)]
+enum ExtractResult {
+    /// `artifact_path` was not a recognized multi-file archive. The caller should
+    /// skip the fallback.
+    NotAnArchive,
+    /// The archive was extracted. `tmp` must outlive the use of `members` — once it
+    /// drops, the extracted files disappear.
+    Extracted {
+        tmp: tempfile::TempDir,
+        members: Vec<PathBuf>,
+    },
+}
+
+/// Extract every regular file from `archive_path` into a fresh temp directory.
+///
+/// Pure helper used by [`verify_attestation_via_archive_contents`] — factored out so the
+/// extraction + enumeration logic is testable without making any GitHub API calls.
+/// `strip_components` is always 0: the verbatim archive layout is what the publisher
+/// signed over, and stripping would silently change member digests.
+///
+/// Returns [`ExtractResult::NotAnArchive`] when `archive_path` doesn't look like a
+/// supported multi-file archive (raw binary, single-file `.gz`/`.xz`, unknown
+/// extension). This is distinct from a successful extraction that yields zero members,
+/// which is reported as `Verification` error by the caller's policy.
+fn extract_archive_members(archive_path: &Path) -> AttestationResult<ExtractResult> {
+    let Some(format) = archive_format_for_path(archive_path) else {
+        return Ok(ExtractResult::NotAnArchive);
+    };
+    let tmp = tempfile::tempdir().map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
+    let opts = TarOptions {
+        strip_components: 0,
+        ..TarOptions::new(format)
+    };
+    crate::file::untar(archive_path, tmp.path(), &opts).map_err(|e| {
+        // Preserve the underlying eyre message verbatim — extraction failures are the
+        // kind of thing users will paste into issues, and the default `Report` Display
+        // is more informative than "Io error: …".
+        AttestationError::UnsupportedFormat(format!(
+            "attestation fallback failed to extract archive {}: {e}",
+            archive_path.display()
+        ))
+    })?;
+    let members = list_regular_files(tmp.path()).map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
+    Ok(ExtractResult::Extracted { tmp, members })
+}
+
+/// Map a per-member verification result into the policy-level outcome.
+///

+            return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
+        }
+        ExtractResult::Extracted { tmp, members } => (tmp, members),
+    };
+
+    debug!(
+        "attestation fallback: verifying {} regular file(s) extracted from {}",
+        members.len(),
+        artifact_path.display()
+    );
+
+    let root = tmp.path();
+    let mut outcomes: Vec<(String, ArchiveMemberOutcome)> = Vec::with_capacity(members.len());
+    for member in &members {
+        let display = relative_display_name(member, root);
+        let result = verify_attestation_blob(member, owner, repo, expected_workflow, api_url).await;
+        outcomes.push((display, result_to_outcome(result)));
+    }
+
+    let result = aggregate_archive_member_outcomes(outcomes);
+    match &result {
+        Ok(true) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback succeeded: every member of {} verified",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+        Ok(false) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback reported verification false for {}",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+        Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback found no attestations for any member of {}",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+        Err(e) => debug!(
+            "attestation fallback failed for {}: {e}",
+            artifact_path.display()
+        ),
+    }
+    result
+}
+
+/// Result of [`extract_archive_members`]: either a real archive was extracted (with the
+/// temp dir held alive for the caller), or the path wasn't an archive at all.
+#[derive(Debug)]
+enum ExtractResult {
+    /// `artifact_path` was not a recognized multi-file archive. The caller should
+    /// skip the fallback.
+    NotAnArchive,
+    /// The archive was extracted. `tmp` must outlive the use of `members` — once it
+    /// drops, the extracted files disappear.
+    Extracted {
+        tmp: tempfile::TempDir,
+        members: Vec<PathBuf>,
+    },
+}
+
+/// Extract every regular file from `archive_path` into a fresh temp directory.
+///
+/// Pure helper used by [`verify_attestation_via_archive_contents`] — factored out so the
+/// extraction + enumeration logic is testable without making any GitHub API calls.
+/// `strip_components` is always 0: the verbatim archive layout is what the publisher
+/// signed over, and stripping would silently change member digests.
+///
+/// Returns [`ExtractResult::NotAnArchive`] when `archive_path` doesn't look like a
+/// supported multi-file archive (raw binary, single-file `.gz`/`.xz`, unknown
+/// extension). This is distinct from a successful extraction that yields zero members,
+/// which is reported as `Verification` error by the caller's policy.
+fn extract_archive_members(archive_path: &Path) -> AttestationResult<ExtractResult> {
+    let Some(format) = archive_format_for_path(archive_path) else {
+        return Ok(ExtractResult::NotAnArchive);
+    };
+    let tmp = tempfile::tempdir().map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
+    let opts = TarOptions {
+        strip_components: 0,
+        ..TarOptions::new(format)
+    };
+    crate::file::untar(archive_path, tmp.path(), &opts).map_err(|e| {
+        // Preserve the underlying eyre message verbatim — extraction failures are the
+        // kind of thing users will paste into issues, and the default `Report` Display
+        // is more informative than "Io error: …".
+        AttestationError::UnsupportedFormat(format!(
+            "attestation fallback failed to extract archive {}: {e}",
+            archive_path.display()
+        ))
+    })?;
+    let members = list_regular_files(tmp.path()).map_err(AttestationError::Io)?;
+    Ok(ExtractResult::Extracted { tmp, members })
+}
+
+/// Map a per-member verification result into the policy-level outcome.
+///

part.text:
The diff looks good. Let me also verify my implementation handles one more important edge case: what happens if the api_url is for GitHub Enterprise where attestations aren't supported? 

Looking back at the call sites:
- `src/backend/github.rs`: `try_verify_github_attestations` only calls `verify_attestation` if `attestations_supported(&api_url)` is true.
- `src/backend/aqua.rs`: `run_github_attestation_check` calls `verify_attestation` without checking. But the aqua backend gates with `settings.aqua.github_attestations`.

So if api_url is GHE (where the attestations endpoint doesn't exist), the underlying `mise_sigstore::verify_github_attestation_with_base_url` would return an API error (404 or similar), which would propagate as `Err(e)` (not `NoAttestations`). My fallback would then NOT trigger (correct — falling back after a real error is forbidden).

But wait — what if the GHE server returns 404 specifically? Let me check what mise-sigstore does on 404:

```rust
if response.status() == reqwest::StatusCode::NOT_FOUND {
    return Ok(vec![]);
}
```

And then:
```rust
if attestations.is_empty() {
    return Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations);
}
```

So on GHE where the endpoint doesn't exist, the API returns 404, mise-sigstore returns empty vec, which becomes NoAttestations. This WOULD trigger my fallback!

But the call site `try_verify_github_attestations` has:
```rust
if !skip_attestations
    && settings.github_attestations
    && settings.github.github_attestations
    && attestations_supported(&api_url)  // <-- this gate
{
    // ... calls verify_attestation
}
```

So `verify_attestation` is only called when `attestations_supported(&api_url)` is true (i.e., public GitHub). For GHE, the call site doesn't even call `verify_attestation` — so my fallback never gets a chance to trigger. Good.

But what about the aqua backend? Let me re-check.

In `aqua.rs:1151`:
```rust
crate::github::sigstore::verify_attestation(
    artifact_path,
    &pkg.repo_owner,
    &pkg.repo_name,
    signer_workflow.as_deref(),
    None,  // <-- api_url is None, defaults to api.github.com
)
```

The api_url is `None`, which defaults to public GitHub. So no GHE concern.

In github.rs:1700 (`try_verify_github_attestations`):
```rust
crate::github::sigstore::verify_attestation(
    file_path,
    owner,
    repo_name,
    None,
    Some(api_url),  // <-- explicit api_url
)
```

But this is gated by `attestations_supported(&api_url)` at the call site. So if api_url is GHE, this is never called.

In github.rs:705 (`verify_provenance_at_lock_time`):
```rust
if !attestations_supported(api_url) && let Some(expected) = expected_provenance && expected.is_github_attestations() {
    return Err(...);
}
```

That's a different check. But the actual call to `verify_attestation` at line 705 is gated by:
```rust
if settings.github_attestations
    && settings.github.github_attestations
    && attestations_supported(api_url)
{
    // ... calls verify_attestation
}
```

Yes, also gated. Good.

So in all call sites, `verify_attestation` is only called when api_url is the public GitHub. My fallback can safely assume the API is reachable. Good.

OK, I'm satisfied with the implementation. Let me also do a final review by running the full sigstore test suite and confirming everything passes.

Actually let me also think about one more potential issue. The fallback path uses `tempfile::tempdir()` which creates a temp dir under `$TMPDIR` (typically `/tmp`). If `/tmp` is mounted `noexec`, the extracted files can still be read for digest computation (which is all we do with them). We don't execute them. So noexec is fine.

If the temp dir is on a different filesystem than the original archive, that's fine — we're just reading bytes.

If the system has very limited disk space, extraction could fail. That's reported as `AttestationError::UnsupportedFormat` (currently mapped to that variant because that's where I put the eyre conversion). Hmm, that's not ideal semantically — extraction failure due to disk space isn't really "unsupported format". Let me reconsider.

Looking at the AttestationError variants:
- Api
- Verification
- UnsupportedFormat
- NoAttestations
- Io
- Http
- Json
- Sigstore

For extraction failures, `Io` would be the most accurate. Let me change the mapping.

Actually wait — `crate::file::untar` returns `eyre::Report`, not `std::io::Error`. So I can't directly use `AttestationError::Io`. But I could either:
(a) Use `UnsupportedFormat` with a clear message (current behavior)
(b) Use `Verification` (semantically wrong)
(c) Use `Sigstore` (definitely wrong)
(d) Add a new variant (overkill)

I think (a) is fine because:
1. The message clearly says "attestation fallback failed to extract archive ... : <reason>"
2. Callers treat all non-NoAttestations errors as hard failures anyway
3. The exact variant doesn't matter for behavior, only the message does

Let me keep (a) but improve the comment. Actually the current code is fine. Let me leave it.

OK, let me finalize and commit.

One more thing: let me check the message in the aggregate function. The format is:
"per-file attestation verification failed for {failures.len()}/{total} archive member(s); every regular file inside the archive must carry a valid GitHub attestation: {failures}"

Let me make sure the failures.len() is what we want. If we have 5 members and 2 fail, the message says "2/5". That's clear: 2 out of 5 failed. Good.

OK let me commit.

part.text:
There's a subtle issue! `is_slsa_format_issue` is used to decide if an SLSA verification error should be soft-handled (just log and continue) vs hard-fail. It treats `UnsupportedFormat` as a "format issue" — i.e., non-fatal, fall through to alternate paths.

My extraction failure error is `UnsupportedFormat`. If this propagated to an SLSA path, it would be mis-classified as a format issue. But:
1. My extraction error is in the github-attestations path, not the SLSA path.
2. The github-attestations call sites treat `UnsupportedFormat` as a hard error (look at github.rs:1700):

```rust
Err(crate::github::sigstore::AttestationError::NoAttestations) => {
    Err(VerificationStatus::NoAttestations)
}
Err(e) => Err(VerificationStatus::Error(e.to_string())),
```

So `UnsupportedFormat` becomes `VerificationStatus::Error`, which becomes a hard error at the call site. Good.

But wait — in the github backend's `verify_attestations_or_slsa`:
```rust
Err(VerificationStatus::Error(e)) => {
    // Error during verification - hard error
    return Err(eyre::eyre!(
        "GitHub artifact attestations verification error for {tv}: {e}"
    ));
}
```

Yes, hard error. Good.

So my mapping is OK behaviorally — extraction failures become hard errors at the call sites. The semantic name `UnsupportedFormat` is a bit awkward (it could also be a disk space issue) but the message is clear.

Hmm, but actually let me reconsider. The `UnsupportedFormat` variant is treated specially by `is_slsa_format_issue` which is used for the SLSA path. My error wouldn't reach that path (it's a github-attestations path error), but it's still confusing semantically.

Let me think about whether there's a better variant:
- Io: would be most accurate for disk errors, but `untar` returns eyre::Report not io::Error
- Verification: the existing semantic is "attestation existed but verification failed" — not right
- Api: not right
- Sigstore: not right

None are perfect. The least-bad option is probably `Verification` since "we couldn't verify the archive" is a kind of verification failure. Actually let me change it to `Verification` since that's what callers fall back to as "hard error" without any special handling.

Wait, but `Verification` is used for "attestation existed but verification failed". For extraction failure (a different concept), using `Verification` could be misleading. Hmm.

Actually you know what, let me just keep `UnsupportedFormat` since the existing pattern in sigstore.rs (the mise-sigstore lib) uses it for "this isn't a format we recognize" which is conceptually close to "this archive couldn't be parsed/extracted". The message is what matters for users.

OK, leaving it. Let me finalize.

Actually, looking once more, I realize I want to double-check one thing. My code path:

```rust
let result = aggregate_archive_member_outcomes(outcomes);
match &result {
    Ok(true) => debug!(...),
    Ok(false) => debug!(...),
    Err(AttestationError::NoAttestations) => debug!(...),
    Err(e) => debug!(...),
}
result
```

`aggregate_archive_member_outcomes` only returns `Ok(true)`, `Err(NoAttestations)`, or `Err(Verification)`. So `Ok(false)` is unreachable. Let me clean this up — the compiler required it because `AttestationResult<bool>` includes Ok(false) as a possibility, but I can just collapse Ok(false) into the Ok(true) branch since aggregate can't return it. Actually no — the compiler will complain. Let me leave it as a defensive `Ok(false)` arm.

Actually it's fine as-is. The compiler is happy and the code is defensive.

Let me do the final commit now.
The mapping is correct given callers' handling. Let me run one final check — the full unit test suite for the file plus a final compile check:

part.text:
Now I'll commit. Per AGENTS.md, commits must follow conventional commit format. The change is a security feature adding per-file archive attestation fallback. Type should be `feat` (new feature). Scope: the change is in `src/github/sigstore.rs` which is part of the github subsystem, but the feature is about attestation verification specifically. The scope options listed include `github`. input.command:
cd /app && git commit -m "feat(github): accept per-file archive attestations as fail-closed fallback

Some release pipelines sign each file inside a release archive rather than
the archive blob itself. Previously, such releases had no path to GitHub
artifact attestation verification in mise -- the archive digest had no
attestation, so verification reported NoAttestations and fell through to
other mechanisms.

verify_attestation now preserves archive-level (blob digest) verification
as the first stage and adds a fail-closed per-file fallback: when the
archive's own digest has no attestation, mise extracts it to a temp
directory and verifies every regular file inside individually.

Policy (per aggregate_archive_member_outcomes, pinned by tests):
- Every regular file inside the archive must carry a valid attestation.
  A single unattested file (README, LICENSE, smuggled binary, ...) rejects
  the whole archive. This is the only policy with no smuggling surface.
- If no inner file has any attestation, surface NoAttestations so callers
  fall through to SLSA / checksums unchanged.
- Any error other than NoAttestations from archive-level verification is
  propagated without attempting a fallback -- retrying after a real
  cryptographic failure would be guessing toward permissiveness.

The fallback only applies to recognized multi-file archive formats
(tar.gz/zip/tar.xz/tar.bz2/tar.zst/tar/7z). Raw binaries and single-file
compressions (.gz/.xz) are unaffected. Symlinks are never followed during
member enumeration, so an adversarial archive cannot leak host files into
the digest check or cause infinite recursion.

The decision to require every regular file (not just executables or
configured bin paths) is a deliberate trust-boundary choice: it eliminates
the smuggling vector where an attacker substitutes an unattested file that
mise later executes. Relaxing later is straightforward; tightening after
ship is not.

The fallback is transparent to all three call sites
(try_verify_github_attestations, verify_provenance_at_lock_time,
run_github_attestation_check) since they all go through the
verify_attestation chokepoint -- no per-call-site changes needed.

35 new focused tests pin the policy, result mapping, format detection,
symlink safety, and end-to-end extraction without hitting the network." 2>&1 | tail -10

part.text:
Done. Summary of the change (commit `450341d`):

**Approach:** Made `verify_attestation` (the single chokepoint for GitHub attestation verification in `src/github/sigstore.rs`) preserve archive-level verification as stage 1 and add a fail-closed per-file archive fallback as stage 2. The fallback only triggers when stage 1 returns `NoAttestations`; any other result (success, hard verification failure, network error) is propagated verbatim — retrying after a real failure would be guessing toward permissiveness.

**Trust boundary (per your "Option A" choice):** every regular file inside the archive must carry a valid attestation. A single unattested file rejects the whole archive. The only soft path is when no inner file has any attestation at all (so callers fall through to SLSA / checksums unchanged).

**Hygiene details:**
- `strip_components=0` so member digests match what the publisher signed.
- `symlink_metadata` everywhere, so symlinks (to `/etc/passwd`, to siblings, into loops) cannot leak foreign bytes into the digest check or cause infinite recursion.
- Hidden files are included; the policy is strict by design.

**Tests (35 new, all passing):** policy aggregation pinned for every case (smuggling case is load-bearing), result→outcome mapping, format detection (incl. parent dirs and case), file enumeration including 3 symlink-safety cases (Unix), end-to-end tar.gz/zip extraction with real archive bytes built in-memory.

**Verification:** `cargo test --bin mise github::sigstore::` (39 pass), `cargo test --bin mise backend::` (247 pass, no regressions to existing call sites), `cargo clippy --bin mise --tests` and `cargo fmt --check` clean.
